title: Representing Time in Networks
concept: We need architectures that process sequences step-by-step, carrying a hidden state that acts as the network's memory.

exercises.prompt: How do neural networks process data that changes over time, like music or text?

options.text: By ignoring the order of the inputs.
By processing inputs step-by-step and keeping a memory.

exercises.explain: To understand sequences like sentences or music, a network must process data step-by-step and remember what came before.

cards.title: Time Needs Memory
cards.body: Standard neural networks look at an image or a number and make a guess. But what if we want to process a video, a sentence, or a song? These are sequences where time matters, and we need a network that remembers what just happened!

cards.title: What is a Hidden State?
cards.body: To process time, networks use a hidden state. This is a bundle of numbers that acts like a working memory. As the network looks at each item in a sequence, it updates this hidden state so it remembers what it has seen so far.

cards.title: The Storybook Memory
cards.body: Think of a network with memory like reading a book. When you read page 10, you don't forget everything from pages 1 to 9. Instead, you carry the story's context in your mind to make sense of the current page. If you read each page in isolation without memory, the story would make no sense!

cards.title: Predicting the Next Word
cards.body: When you type 'How are...', a network predicts 'you'. It does this because its hidden state remembers 'How' and 'are' from the previous steps, combining that past information with the current moment to guess the next word.

cards.title: The 'All at Once' Myth
cards.body: People often think networks need to see a whole sequence all at once to understand it. In reality, they can process it one tiny step at a time, updating their memory along the way. This keeps things efficient and fast!

cards.title: Memory vs No Memory
cards.body: Compare a standard network that treats every step as a brand new day, versus a sequential network that remembers the past.

left.text: Processes inputs in isolation. No memory of what happened a second ago.

right.text: Uses a hidden state to carry memory forward step-by-step.

exercises.prompt: How do sequence-based networks carry information from past steps to future steps?

options.text: They process the sequence backwards first.
They store every past input in a giant database.
They carry a hidden state that updates at each step.
They freeze time while making calculations.

exercises.explain: Standard feedforward networks process each input independently and do not carry a hidden state across a sequence, making them unsuitable for tracking time.

exercises.prompt: A hidden state is used to carry memory from one step to the next.
exercises.explain: A hidden state is essential because it allows the network to maintain a memory of past inputs in a sequence.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe how networks keep track of time.
exercises.sentence: The hidden state acts as the network's own internal memory.
exercises.explain: The hidden state acts as the network's memory, allowing it to remember past inputs in a sequence.

exercises.prompt: Which of these AI systems is designed to represent time and sequences properly?

options.text: Model A: It looks at every word in a sentence individually and forgets them instantly.
Model B: It processes one word at a time, updating a memory state at each step.
Model C: It ignores the order of words and just counts how many letters are present.

exercises.explain: Model B represents a sequence-based network because it uses a memory system (hidden state) to connect different steps in time.

exercises.prompt: What happens if we process a sequence step-by-step but completely remove the hidden state?

options.text: It makes the network train much faster.
The network forgets past steps and processes each input in isolation.
It forces the network to only look at the very last word.
It automatically fixes all grammar mistakes in the data.

exercises.explain: Without a hidden state to pass information from one step to the next, a network cannot remember what happened previously in a sequence. This makes it impossible to process context in text, speech, or time-series data.
